title: Rabbit dental health check
subtitle: How to check your rabbit's front teeth and what problems to look for.
excerpt: Rabbit teeth grow throughout life and wear only if the diet provides the right material to grind against. Dental problems are among the most common reasons rabbits are presented to vets; and most are caused by diet. A quick monthly check catches problems early.
sourceNotes: RSPCA, rabbit dental care guidance. RWAF (Rabbit Welfare Association and Fund), dental health factsheet. British Rabbit Council, health and welfare notes.

content.text: A rabbit has two pairs of upper incisor teeth (one pair in front of the other) and one pair of lower incisors, plus

further back. The incisors are the only teeth you can check at home without equipment. A monthly incisor check, combined with monitoring body weight and hay consumption, catches most dental problems while they are still correctable.

Hold the rabbit securely against your body or on a non-slip surface. Gently lift the upper lip with your thumb to expose the front incisors. The upper pair should overhang the lower pair slightly. The teeth should be white or off-white, smooth, with no chips or cracks, and their cutting edges should be clean. The two upper front teeth should be even in length; if one appears shorter than the other, it may be broken at the root.

, the teeth may grow at an angle rather than straight down. The lower incisors may protrude in front of the upper pair rather than sitting behind them. Overgrown incisors that are not being worn normally will curve outward and eventually cannot be used for grasping food. This is a vet referral; do not attempt to trim teeth at home.
When to suspect cheek tooth problems

Cheek teeth problems are harder to spot. Watch for: hay consumption falling off, the rabbit picking up and dropping food, weight loss over several weeks, wet fur under the chin (from drooling), or a swelling on the jaw or below the eye. These all warrant a vet examination. Cheek tooth spurs (sharp points on the back teeth that dig into the tongue or cheek) are painful and prevent normal eating but are only visible under anaesthesia.

A hay-based diet that requires constant chewing is the primary prevention. Gnawing on hay, dried twigs from safe wood species (apple, willow, hazel), or untreated wooden objects gives additional wear. The physical act of grinding long-stemmed material wears the cheek teeth as the jaw moves in a lateral motion that pelleted or soft food does not produce.
